figures.caption: Figure 1. Performance of our approach in terms of individual and mean F1 scores on the OpenSDI benchmark for AI-generated and -inpainted images [51]. Training only on SD1.5 [38], we achieve state-of-the-art performance and generalization on unseen generator models (SD2.1 [38], SD3 [11], SDXL [33], Flux [21]), surpassing established methods using only a frozen VFM [4] and our tunable attention pooling (TAP).

Table 3. Performance Comparison on the Chameleon [53] dataset. We benchmark the ViT-L and ViT-G variants of the PE-Core vision foundation model [4] and compare accuracy (Acc), accuracy on generated images (F-Acc), and accuracy on real images (R-Acc) with and without tunable attention pooling (TAP) features. All methods are trained using the SD v1.4 training set from GenImage [64]. Best result is shown in bold, while second best is underlined.

Table 2. Performance comparison on the OpenSDI [51] dataset for generated and inpainted images. We benchmark the ViT-L and ViT-G variants of the PE-Core vision foundation model [4] and compare F1-score and accuracy with and without tunable attention pooling (TAP) features against prior methods. All methods are trained on the SD v1.5 training set of OpenSDI. Best results are in bold, while second best are underlined.

Figure 2. t-SNE analysis of the feature spaces of three pretrained foundational ViT variants: CLIP-ViT-L/14 [35], SIGLIP2-ViT-SO400MNaFlex/16 [46], and PE-Core-ViT-G/14 [4]. Top row shows the cls token output of the frozen visual encoders, while bottom row shows the global-pooled gpl token obtained with our tunable attention pooling (TAP) approach. Features are obtained from the Stable Diffusion 3 test set of OpenSDI [51]

Table 4. Comparison of visual encoders from various vision foundation model families on OpenSDI [51]. † indicates a variable-resolution model. Best results are in bold, while second best are underlined.

Figure 3. Method Overview. Classical semantic feature extraction for AIGI detection relies on the outdated prior of the original CLIP ViT [35]. In addition, many approaches discard the patch tokens and utilize only the cls token. Our approach updates the semantic feature extraction process by swapping the CLIP ViT with an encoder from a vision foundation model (VFM). Moreover, we take advantage of the full output sequence by employing tunable attention pooling (TAP), reducing the dimensionality while also obtaining a refined global embedding gpl, yielding improvements over a cls-token-only approach.

Table 5. Performance comparison of various CLIP-style vision foundation models on OpenSDI [51], evaluated with and without our tunable attention pooling (TAP). Best results are in bold, while second best are underlined.

Table 1. Performance comparison on the GenImage [64] dataset. We benchmark the ViT-L and ViT-G variants of the PE-Core vision foundation model [4] and compare accuracy with and without tunable attention pooling (TAP) features. All methods were trained on generated images sourced from SD v1.4 [38] with real images from ImageNet [8]. Results with ’†’ and ’‡’ are sourced from AIDE [53] and OMAT [61], respectively. Best results are in bold, while second best are underlined.
